title: Concept of IoT & Architecture | True/False Quiz
heading: IoT Architecture & Concepts: True/False Challenge
description: Test your knowledge on IoT architecture layers, XaaS model, analytics types, visualization, constraints, and reference architectures with True/False questions only.

questions.question: In an XaaS model, customers make a one-time payment and handle all maintenance.
questions.explanation: False: XaaS (Anything as a Service) follows a subscription/pay-as-you-go model where the provider handles maintenance, not a one-time payment.

questions.question: Using the same dashboard design for executives, operators, and engineers is a best practice in IoT visualization.
questions.explanation: False: Different roles have different needs. Dashboards should be tailored to each user's requirements and level of detail.

questions.question: In IoT, 'security by design' means security can be added later before deployment.
questions.explanation: False: Security by design means security is integrated from the initial design phase, not added as an afterthought before deployment.

questions.question: Environmental constraints only affect hardware and not software or firmware in IoT systems.
questions.explanation: False: Environmental constraints (temperature, humidity, vibration) affect hardware, software, and firmware design, including power management and error handling.

questions.question: The perception layer in IoT is responsible for sensing data from the environment.
questions.explanation: True: The perception layer (or device layer) includes sensors and actuators that interact directly with the physical environment.

questions.question: The network layer is responsible for data transmission in IoT systems.
questions.explanation: True: The network layer handles communication protocols, routing, and data transmission between devices and the cloud.

questions.question: The processing layer performs data filtering and analytics.
questions.explanation: True: The processing layer (or middleware/platform layer) manages data storage, filtering, aggregation, and analytics.

questions.question: The application layer provides services to end-users.
questions.explanation: True: The application layer delivers IoT services and interfaces to end-users across domains like smart homes, healthcare, and industry.

questions.question: IoT gateways perform protocol translation between devices and cloud systems.
questions.explanation: True: Gateways translate between different protocols (e.g., Zigbee to MQTT), aggregate data, and provide local processing.

questions.question: XaaS converts capital expenditure (CapEx) into operational expenditure (OpEx).
questions.explanation: True: XaaS shifts costs from upfront CapEx (buying hardware/software) to ongoing OpEx (subscription/pay-as-you-go).

questions.question: Predictive analytics answers the question 'What should we do?'.
questions.explanation: False: Predictive analytics answers 'What will happen?' (forecasting). Prescriptive analytics answers 'What should we do?' (recommendations).

questions.question: Prescriptive analytics suggests actions based on predictions.
questions.explanation: True: Prescriptive analytics uses optimization and simulation to recommend specific actions based on predictive insights.

questions.question: Line charts are useful for showing time-based trends in IoT data.
questions.explanation: True: Line charts effectively display continuous data trends over time, such as temperature or sensor readings.

questions.question: Maps are useful for representing location-based IoT data.
questions.explanation: True: Maps visualize geospatial IoT data like fleet tracking, environmental monitoring, and asset localization.

questions.question: The DIKW pyramid includes Data, Information, Knowledge, and Wisdom.
questions.explanation: True: The DIKW hierarchy represents the progression from raw Data to meaningful Information to actionable Knowledge to Wisdom.

questions.question: The 'battery barrier' refers to unlimited power availability in IoT devices.
questions.explanation: False: The battery barrier refers to the limited energy capacity in IoT devices, constraining processing, communication, and lifetime.

questions.question: Cost constraints become more significant in large-scale IoT deployments.
questions.explanation: True: At scale, per-unit costs (hardware, deployment, maintenance) multiply, making cost optimization critical for feasibility.

questions.question: Industrial Internet Reference Architecture (IIRA) is designed only for smart home devices.
questions.explanation: False: IIRA is designed for industrial IoT systems (manufacturing, energy, transportation), not smart home consumer devices.

questions.question: Autonomous control systems require human intervention for every decision.
questions.explanation: False: Autonomous control systems make and execute decisions independently without human intervention for routine operations.

questions.question: IoT systems can operate without any network connectivity.
questions.explanation: False: While some local processing is possible, IoT systems fundamentally rely on network connectivity for data transmission and coordination.
